Old pods query `email_address` which no longer exists - PostgreSQL returns column does not exist; every request fails. New pods are fine but only handle ~50% of traffic. 5xx rate spikes to 47%. Engineer notices alarms 2 min in, panics, rolls back deploy - now 6 old pods all querying nonexistent column. Has to roll back the migration (which means a manual rename back - now duplicate panic), but in the meantime 8 minutes of broken traffic = 240k failed API calls, ~50k customer-facing errors, three angry account managers, and a postmortem that costs the team a week. With expand/contract, this scenario simply cannot happen because both column names work the entire time.

title: Convert 800-line .gitlab-ci.yml into parent-child pipelines with DAG and dynamic generation
scenario_context: Media company with a Node.js/TypeScript monorepo on self-hosted GitLab. The single .gitlab-ci.yml has grown to 832 lines covering 14 services. Every commit triggers all 14 pipelines sequentially even if only one service changed. Median pipeline runs 47 minutes; engineers wait, lose context, ship less. The yaml has so many `rules:` clauses with cargo-culted `if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == 'main'` that nobody understands which jobs run when. Last quarter, a hotfix to one service blocked behind a flaky test in an unrelated service.
requirements: Only build services with changes (path-based detection)
Parallel execution across services with DAG dependencies (needs:)
Pipelines composable across teams without one team's edit breaking another's
Auto-stopped review apps per MR on GKE preview environments
Pipeline definition under 100 lines per service file
Merge-train safe (no race conditions in shared resources)
constraints: Self-hosted GitLab CE 16.x (no Premium features)
Existing 70 environments in GitLab UI - cannot orphan them
Some services share Docker base image - rebuild ordering matters
Runners are autoscaled spot VMs on GCE; jobs can be evicted mid-run

current_state.description: Monolithic .gitlab-ci.yml runs every stage for every service on every commit. Sequential stages, no DAG, broken caching across runners.

files.content:
stages: [lint, test, build, deploy]

lint:user-service:
  stage: lint
  script: cd services/user && npm ci && npm run lint

test:user-service:
  stage: test
  script: cd services/user && npm ci && npm test

build:user-service:
  stage: build
  script: cd services/user && docker build -t registry/user:$CI_COMMIT_SHA . && docker push

# ... 800 more lines repeating for 13 other services

deploy:user-service:
  stage: deploy
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == 'main'
  script: kubectl set image deployment/user-service user=registry/user:$CI_COMMIT_SHA
issues: Sequential stages - lint of svc 1 blocks test of svc 14
npm ci runs in every job - no shared cache
Docker login implicit, no GCR auth via short-lived token
No path-based filtering - touches in docs/ rebuild everything
kubectl set image directly - no manifest tracking, no rollback

reasoning:
1. WHY IT FAILS
Monolithic CI is the natural attractor state for a growing monorepo: each team adds 'just one more job' until the file is unreadable. The DAG inefficiency is the visible pain (47-min runs); the maintenance hazard is the hidden cost (nobody changes the yaml because they don't trust they won't break adjacent services).

Sequential stages force a barrier: ALL lint jobs finish before ANY test job starts. With 14 services, the slowest lint blocks 13 tests. With `needs:` (DAG), each service's test depends only on its own lint - 14 independent pipelines effectively.

2. DESIGN DECISIONS
Parent-child pipelines:
- One thin parent pipeline (.gitlab-ci.yml, ~50 lines) inspects changed paths via `rules:changes:` and triggers a child pipeline per affected service.
- Each service owns its own /services/<svc>/.gitlab-ci.yml describing lint/test/build/deploy with `needs:` DAG.
- Shared concerns (Docker base image, deploy templates) live in a single /ci/templates/ directory included via `include:local:`.

Dynamic child generation: for services with template-driven matrix (e.g., 'build for 3 region clusters'), use a generator job that produces a child pipeline yaml from a template + variables. Triggered via `trigger:include:artifact:`.

DAG with `needs:`:
- lint and test run in parallel (both depend on npm-install)
- build depends on lint+test passing
- deploy depends on build
- Each service's chain is independent; 14 services running concurrently

Review apps: MR pipeline deploys to a GKE namespace `preview-mr-${CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID}-${service}`, registers a GitLab environment with `on_stop:` job that deletes the namespace when MR is closed/merged or 7 days idle. Uses `environment:auto_stop_in: 7 days`.

Caching strategy: per-service node_modules cache keyed by `package-lock.json` hash, scoped via `cache:key:files:`. Cache pulled on lint/test, pulled+pushed on the install job. Runners have a shared object-storage cache backend (GCS bucket) so different VMs share cache.

Spot eviction safety: `retry: 2` on jobs likely to be evicted (long-running tests); `interruptible: true` on lint/test jobs so a new commit cancels stale runs.

3. REJECTED ALTERNATIVES
- Switching to GitHub Actions: tooling migration of this size is months of work; current pain doesn't justify.
- Buildkite: requires self-hosted control plane; team can't sustain.
- Generated single yaml from a script: still produces a giant file; loses the per-service ownership boundary.
- only/except instead of rules: deprecated; rules has clearer semantics including changes: filters.
- Always running all services 'for safety': what we're escaping; correctness via change-detection is achievable.

4. FAILURE MODES PREVENTED
- One slow service blocks all: DAG eliminates the barrier.
- Forgotten env cleanup: auto_stop_in tears down review apps.
- Cache invalidation across runners: shared GCS cache backend keyed on lockfile hash.
- Spot eviction loses work: interruptible+retry handles transient infra.
- Service drift: per-service yaml under that service's CODEOWNERS - each team owns its pipeline.

5. MIGRATION PATH
Week 1: Build parent pipeline + 1 service (lowest-risk) migrated. Run both old and new in parallel; compare timing.
Week 2: Migrate 5 more services. Old jobs disabled, new active.
Week 3: Remaining 8 services migrated. Delete old monolith.
Week 4: Enable shared GCS cache; measure cache hit improvement.
Week 5: Add review app auto-stop. Run a cleanup script to delete orphaned envs from the old system.

6. OPERATIONAL CONSIDERATIONS
Each team owns /services/<svc>/.gitlab-ci.yml via CODEOWNERS. Platform team owns /ci/templates/. Adding a new service = adding a directory + 30-line yaml + 5-line entry in parent pipeline.

target_architecture.description: Parent pipeline triggers per-service child pipelines based on path changes. Each child uses DAG (needs:) for parallel execution. Shared templates centralize Docker/deploy patterns. Review apps auto-stop. GCS-backed cache shared across runners.
target_architecture.diagram_description:
.gitlab-ci.yml (parent, ~50 lines)
  ├─ trigger services/user/.gitlab-ci.yml (if services/user/** changed)
  ├─ trigger services/billing/.gitlab-ci.yml (if services/billing/** changed)
  └─ ... 12 more
Each child pipeline:
  install ──► lint ──┐
     │              ├─► build ──► deploy
     └──► test ─────┘
MR pipelines additionally:
  build ──► review-app-deploy (registers GitLab environment with on_stop:cleanup-review)

solution_files.content:
include:
  - local: '/ci/templates/global.yml'

stages: [trigger]

variables:
  GIT_DEPTH: '50'
  GIT_STRATEGY: fetch

workflow:
  rules:
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == 'merge_request_event'
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == 'main'
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_TAG

.trigger-service: &trigger-service
  stage: trigger
  trigger:
    include:
      - local: 'services/$SERVICE/.gitlab-ci.yml'
    strategy: depend

user-service:
  <<: *trigger-service
  variables: { SERVICE: user }
  rules:
    - changes:
        compare_to: 'refs/heads/main'
        paths: ['services/user/**/*', 'shared/**/*', 'ci/templates/**/*']

billing-service:
  <<: *trigger-service
  variables: { SERVICE: billing }
  rules:
    - changes:
        compare_to: 'refs/heads/main'
        paths: ['services/billing/**/*', 'shared/**/*', 'ci/templates/**/*']

# ... 12 more service triggers

generate-matrix-jobs:
  stage: trigger
  image: alpine:3.19
  script:
    - apk add --no-cache jq
    - ./ci/scripts/generate-matrix-pipeline.sh > generated-matrix.yml
  artifacts:
    paths: [generated-matrix.yml]
  rules:
    - changes:
        paths: ['services/notifications/regions/*.yaml']

trigger-matrix:
  stage: trigger
  needs: [generate-matrix-jobs]
  trigger:
    include:
      - artifact: generated-matrix.yml
        job: generate-matrix-jobs
    strategy: depend
solution_files.purpose: Parent pipeline: path-aware triggers per service, dynamic child generation for matrix workloads
key_decisions: YAML anchors (&trigger-service) avoid copy-paste across services
rules:changes:compare_to: 'refs/heads/main' - prevents 'all jobs run on first commit of branch' false positives
strategy: depend - parent pipeline status reflects child pipeline status
workflow:rules at top level prevents duplicate MR+branch pipelines
Dynamic generation example shows pattern without forcing it on every service

solution_files.content:
.node-cache:
  cache:
    key:
      files:
        - '$SERVICE_PATH/package-lock.json'
    paths:
      - '$SERVICE_PATH/node_modules'
      - '$SERVICE_PATH/.npm'
    policy: pull

.node-install:
  extends: .node-cache
  image: node:20.11.1-bookworm-slim@sha256:6a72e0e1cd6a96b81e7ea63c1bd61f4c43e6e4a8b4f80b8c1d7e2c3e3d6a3b8c
  cache:
    policy: pull-push
  before_script:
    - cd $SERVICE_PATH
  script:
    - npm ci --prefer-offline --cache .npm --no-audit
  interruptible: true
  timeout: 5m

.docker-build:
  image: docker:25-cli
  services:
    - docker:25-dind
  variables:
    DOCKER_TLS_CERTDIR: /certs
    DOCKER_BUILDKIT: '1'
  before_script:
    - echo "$GCR_SA_KEY" | docker login -u _json_key --password-stdin https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev
  retry:
    max: 2
    when: [runner_system_failure, stuck_or_timeout_failure]

.deploy-template:
  image: google/cloud-sdk:467.0.0-alpine
  before_script:
    - echo "$GKE_SA_KEY" | base64 -d > /tmp/key.json
    - gcloud auth activate-service-account --key-file=/tmp/key.json
    - gcloud container clusters get-credentials prod-cluster --region us-central1 --project acme-prod
  resource_group: $CI_ENVIRONMENT_NAME
solution_files.purpose: Shared templates - cache, install, docker, deploy patterns
key_decisions: cache:key:files:lockfile - cache busts only when deps change
policy: pull on most jobs, pull-push only on install - install job is the cache source
interruptible: true - new commit cancels stale jobs
resource_group: $CI_ENVIRONMENT_NAME - serializes deploys per environment, prevents racing kubectl
Docker pinned by digest, GCR auth via base64 SA key (will move to Workload Identity later)

solution_files.content:
variables:
  SERVICE_PATH: services/user
  IMAGE: us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/acme-prod/services/user

stages: [install, verify, build, deploy]

install:
  extends: .node-install
  stage: install

lint:
  extends: .node-cache
  stage: verify
  needs: [install]
  image: node:20.11.1-bookworm-slim
  script:
    - cd $SERVICE_PATH
    - npm run lint
  interruptible: true

test:
  extends: .node-cache
  stage: verify
  needs: [install]
  image: node:20.11.1-bookworm-slim
  services:
    - postgres:16-alpine
  variables:
    POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
    POSTGRES_HOST_AUTH_METHOD: trust
  script:
    - cd $SERVICE_PATH
    - npm test -- --coverage --ci --reporters=default --reporters=jest-junit
  artifacts:
    when: always
    reports:
      junit: $SERVICE_PATH/junit.xml
      coverage_report:
        coverage_format: cobertura
        path: $SERVICE_PATH/coverage/cobertura-coverage.xml
    expire_in: 1 week
  coverage: '/Lines\s*:\s*(\d+\.\d+)%/'
  interruptible: true

build:
  extends: .docker-build
  stage: build
  needs: [lint, test]
  script:
    - cd $SERVICE_PATH
    - docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --provenance true --sbom true -t $IMAGE:$CI_COMMIT_SHA -t $IMAGE:$CI_COMMIT_REF_SLUG --push .
  rules:
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == 'merge_request_event'
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == 'main'

deploy:review:
  extends: .deploy-template
  stage: deploy
  needs: [build]
  environment:
    name: review/user-$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID
    url: https://user-$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID.preview.acme.com
    on_stop: stop:review
    auto_stop_in: 7 days
  variables:
    NS: preview-mr-$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID
  script:
    - kubectl create namespace $NS --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
    - kubectl -n $NS apply -f $SERVICE_PATH/k8s/preview/
    - kubectl -n $NS set image deployment/user user=$IMAGE:$CI_COMMIT_SHA
    - kubectl -n $NS rollout status deployment/user --timeout=5m
  rules:
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == 'merge_request_event'

stop:review:
  extends: .deploy-template
  stage: deploy
  needs: [deploy:review]
  environment:
    name: review/user-$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID
    action: stop
  variables:
    NS: preview-mr-$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID
    GIT_STRATEGY: none
  script:
    - kubectl delete namespace $NS --ignore-not-found --wait=false
  rules:
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == 'merge_request_event'
      when: manual
  allow_failure: true

deploy:prod:
  extends: .deploy-template
  stage: deploy
  needs: [build]
  environment:
    name: production/user
    url: https://api.acme.com
  script:
    - kubectl -n prod set image deployment/user user=$IMAGE:$CI_COMMIT_SHA --record=false
    - kubectl -n prod annotate deployment/user kubernetes.io/change-cause="$CI_COMMIT_SHA $CI_PIPELINE_URL" --overwrite
    - kubectl -n prod rollout status deployment/user --timeout=10m
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == 'main'
  when: manual
solution_files.purpose: Per-service child pipeline with DAG, review apps, and gated prod deploy
key_decisions: needs: explicitly forms DAG - lint and test parallel, both after install
Postgres as a service container - integration test runs against real PG
junit + cobertura artifacts surface test/coverage in GitLab MR UI
environment.on_stop + auto_stop_in - review apps clean up automatically
resource_group: $CI_ENVIRONMENT_NAME (inherited) serializes prod deploys
when: manual on prod deploy - human-gated even with main branch protection
Image tagged with both SHA and ref_slug - allows pin or follow-branch consumers

security_considerations: Service account keys still used for GCR/GKE - documented as Week 6 follow-up to migrate to Workload Identity (GitLab JWT → GCP). Until then, SA keys are scoped: GCR push role can push to one Artifact Registry repo per service; GKE deploy role can deploy to one namespace. Docker images use BuildKit provenance + SBOM. resource_group prevents concurrent deploy races - one prod deploy at a time. Manual gate on prod with required approval (configured in GitLab project settings).
observability_hooks: GitLab exposes pipeline duration, job duration, success rate per job/service via the metrics API. Scrape into Prometheus with a custom exporter. Alert on (a) pipeline median duration regression >20% week-over-week, (b) cache hit rate below 70%, (c) review-app namespace count exceeding 100 (signal of stop:review failures). Add a coverage trend chart per service. JSON-log every deploy with service, sha, env, duration, success - ship to BigQuery for change-failure-rate DORA metrics.
cost_analysis: Runner minutes: from ~5500 min/mo (sequential, all services every commit) to ~1100 min/mo (DAG + path filter). At self-hosted spot VM cost ~$0.015/min = $66/mo saved on compute. GCS cache: ~$2/mo storage, negligible egress (intra-region). Review apps: ~$30/mo extra GKE for preview namespaces (auto-stop keeps this bounded). Net: ~$30/mo direct savings + 36-min faster median pipeline x ~400 builds/mo = engineers reclaim ~240 hours/quarter.
rollback_procedure:
1. Per-service pipeline broken: revert /services/<svc>/.gitlab-ci.yml - one team's mess doesn't block others.
2. Parent pipeline broken: rules misconfigured leading to no triggers; check `Pipeline schedules` page for last successful run; revert .gitlab-ci.yml.
3. Shared template breaks all: high blast radius - changes to /ci/templates/ require MR review by platform team + CI runs in shadow mode for 24h before promotion.
4. Bad deploy: kubectl rollout undo deployment/<svc> -n prod - one command, ~30s. Subsequent CI deploy will see the prior image tag as 'current', won't fight it.
5. Decision criteria: rollback the CI change for broken pipeline; rollback the deploy for broken service.
runbook: Adding a new service: copy services/_template/, customize variables.SERVICE_PATH and IMAGE; add a 5-line trigger block to .gitlab-ci.yml.
Cache miss rate spiking: check GCS bucket lifecycle - we keep 30 days; if a cache key changes (lockfile update), old cache expires.

Review app namespace orphaned (stop job failed): script `ci/scripts/cleanup-orphaned-previews.sh` lists namespaces matching preview-mr-* with no open MR, deletes them. Runs weekly via scheduled pipeline.

Pipeline taking longer than expected: check GitLab pipeline graph for which service is the long pole; usually a flaky test in retry loop. Coverage report file size can also bloat artifacts and slow downloads.
Onboarding a runner: register with `--executor docker-machine`, configure GCS cache via /etc/gitlab-runner/config.toml [runners.cache] block pointing to shared bucket.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior 'optimizes' by wrapping every job in `rules: - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH'. Looks tidy. Three weeks later: a security advisory for lodash forces a shared/utils/package.json bump. The bump triggers only the shared/ test job because path rules don't cascade to dependent services. PR merges with 'all green'. The downstream 8 services that consume shared/ have subtly broken behavior (lodash type change), but their CI didn't run. Prod incident at 4am: payments service crashing on every request due to a lodash.get signature change. Postmortem: every service that depends on shared/ should rebuild when shared/ changes. The fix is rules:changes:paths including 'shared/**/*' (which we have), but it's easy to forget when adding services. Real-world insurance: rules-driven CI must include shared paths AND a periodic 'rebuild all services' scheduled pipeline as a safety net.

title: Implement canary deployment with metric-based promotion and automatic rollback
scenario_context: Streaming media platform on EKS, 80M MAU. Currently using blue-green via two ALBs and DNS switching - takes 10 minutes per deploy and customer support hates the cliff-edge cutover that drops session affinity. Past 90 days: 4 incidents where a buggy release caused error spikes within 5 minutes of deploy; rolling back via DNS took another 10 minutes due to TTL. SRE team wants 'progressive delivery': small slice of traffic, watch metrics, promote or roll back automatically.
requirements: Canary receives 5% of traffic at first, ramps to 25%, 50%, 100% over 30 minutes
Automatic promotion only if error rate < 0.5% and p99 latency < 200ms during each step
Automatic rollback if SLO breach during any step (no human in the loop)
Per-user session affinity preserved during canary (no user bounces between v1 and v2 mid-session)
No DNS changes - all traffic shifting via in-cluster L7 routing
Engineer can override and force-promote or force-rollback via Slack command
constraints: Already running Argo CD for GitOps - solution must integrate
Cannot adopt a service mesh (Istio considered too heavy)
Must work for stateful services with WebSocket connections, not just request/response
Argo Rollouts is acceptable; Flagger is acceptable; custom is not

current_state.description: Argo CD applies a Deployment manifest. Cutover via ALB target group switch. No canary, no progressive shifting, no metric-based gating.

files.content:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: stream-api
spec:
  replicas: 40
  selector: {matchLabels: {app: stream-api}}
  template:
    metadata: {labels: {app: stream-api}}
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: ghcr.io/acme/stream-api:v1.42.0
issues: No metrics gating - rely on humans noticing dashboards
Session affinity via ALB stickiness - lost when target group changes

reasoning:
1. WHY CURRENT FAILS
Blue-green at the ALB layer is binary: 100% blue or 100% green. There's no 'observe 5% for 5 minutes' phase. Rollback latency is dominated by ALB target group sync + DNS TTL. The session-affinity loss is structural: when you switch the active target group, ALB stickiness cookies no longer match anything, so users bounce.

Humans-in-the-loop monitoring fails because the noticeable signal often lags the incident: by the time alarms fire, 100% of traffic is already on the bad version and damage is done.

2. DESIGN DECISIONS
Use Argo Rollouts (already in the family with Argo CD) with the Canary strategy. Replace `kind: Deployment` with `kind: Rollout` (drop-in compatible pod spec). Rollouts orchestrates two ReplicaSets (stable + canary) and adjusts traffic weights via the ingress/service-mesh integration of choice. We use the AWS Load Balancer Controller's TargetGroupBinding to do weighted routing at the ALB level - no service mesh needed.

Metric-based analysis: AnalysisTemplate queries Prometheus for `error_rate` and `p99_latency` over each step's duration. If either breaches SLO, rollback. Use Prometheus PromQL directly - no third-party metrics broker.

Session affinity: ALB stickiness via app cookie; we configure the TargetGroupBinding with two target groups (stable + canary) sharing a cookie domain - once a user is bucketed to canary, they stick to canary. New users get distributed by traffic weight.

WebSocket: ALB supports WebSocket; key concern is that a deploy doesn't kill open sockets. Stable pods stay running through entire rollout (Rollouts manages this). Canary pods accept new connections; existing sockets stay on their original pod until the user reconnects (typical for streaming clients). When canary is promoted, stable pods are terminated with graceful drain (see task devops-003 pattern) which forces socket reconnect to canary version.

Slack integration: Argo Rollouts has notification triggers; we wire `RolloutStepCompleted`, `RolloutPaused`, `RolloutAborted` to Slack. Engineers respond with /argo promote or /argo abort - a slack-rollouts-bridge service translates to kubectl argo rollouts.

3. REJECTED ALTERNATIVES
- Istio + Flagger: capable but adds sidecar overhead across 40 pods, plus Istio control plane operational load.
- Linkerd + Flagger: lighter than Istio but still mesh; team isn't there.
- Custom controller: massive build, fragile, no community support.
- Spinnaker: deeply capable but installation/learning curve is months.
- Stay blue-green but add metric gates before cutover: doesn't get progressive shifting; the cliff-edge still exists.

4. FAILURE MODES PREVENTED
- Bad release affects 100% of users: max blast radius is the step weight (5% at first).
- Slow human reaction: metric gates trigger rollback in <60s typically.
- WebSocket disconnects from deploy: graceful drain of stable pods only after canary promoted.
- Session bouncing: stickiness cookies preserved across stable/canary.
- Flaky metric causes false rollback: AnalysisTemplate uses multiple samples; single spike doesn't trip.

5. MIGRATION PATH
Day 1: Install Argo Rollouts controller in cluster. Convert one low-traffic service to Rollout kind, in shadow mode (canary weight 0%, just observe behavior).
Day 3: Run a real canary on the low-traffic service. Force a bad image; verify auto-rollback.
Day 5-10: Migrate stream-api to Rollout. First few rollouts with weights 5→10→25→50→100 and pauseDuration high (30 min per step) to allow human review.
Day 14: Tighten pauseDurations to 5-10 min per step.
Day 21: Enable Slack notifications + slash-command bridge.

6. OPERATIONAL CONSIDERATIONS
SRE team owns AnalysisTemplate definitions (the SLOs are SRE-owned). Service teams own Rollout strategy parameters (step weights, durations). Slack channel #deploys gets real-time updates. On-call runbook covers force-promote, force-rollback, and 'paused longer than expected'.

target_architecture.description: Argo Rollouts manages a stable ReplicaSet and a canary ReplicaSet. Traffic split via AWS ALB target group weights. AnalysisTemplate queries Prometheus at each step; auto-rollback on SLO breach. Slack notifications + control via bot.
target_architecture.diagram_description:
Deploy v2:
  Step 1 (5% canary): canary RS scales up, ALB weighted 95/5 → Analysis queries Prom for 60s → pass: promote
  Step 2 (25%): ALB 75/25 → Analysis 60s → pass: promote
  Step 3 (50%): ALB 50/50 → Analysis 60s → pass: promote
  Step 4 (100%): ALB 0/100 → canary becomes new stable → old RS scaled down with graceful drain
At any step: error_rate > 0.5% or p99 > 200ms → abort rollout, ALB returns to 100/0, canary RS scaled to 0
Slack: each step transition → message in #deploys; rollback → @oncall page

solution_files.content:
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Rollout
metadata:
  name: stream-api
  namespace: prod
spec:
  replicas: 40
  revisionHistoryLimit: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: stream-api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: stream-api
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: ghcr.io/acme/stream-api:v1.42.0
        ports: [{containerPort: 3000, name: http}]
        resources:
          requests: {cpu: 500m, memory: 512Mi}
          limits: {memory: 1Gi}
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet: {path: /healthz/ready, port: 3000}
          periodSeconds: 2
  strategy:
    canary:
      canaryService: stream-api-canary
      stableService: stream-api-stable
      trafficRouting:
        alb:
          ingress: stream-api-ingress
          servicePort: 80
      steps:
      - setWeight: 5
      - pause: {duration: 60s}
      - analysis:
          templates:
          - templateName: canary-slo
          args:
          - name: service
            value: stream-api
          - name: canary-hash
            valueFrom: {podTemplateHashValue: Latest}
      - setWeight: 25
      - pause: {duration: 5m}
      - analysis:
          templates: [{templateName: canary-slo}]
          args:
          - name: service
            value: stream-api
      - setWeight: 50
      - pause: {duration: 10m}
      - analysis:
          templates: [{templateName: canary-slo}]
          args:
          - name: service
            value: stream-api
      - setWeight: 100
      maxUnavailable: 0
      maxSurge: 25%
      abortScaleDownDelaySeconds: 30
solution_files.purpose: Argo Rollouts canary configuration with 4-step progression and analysis gates
key_decisions: 4 progressive steps: 5%/25%/50%/100% - matches industry-standard caution curve
Inline analysis steps run BETWEEN weight changes - failures abort before more traffic flows
ALB traffic routing - no service mesh needed
maxUnavailable: 0 + maxSurge: 25% - never reduce capacity
abortScaleDownDelaySeconds: 30 - delay canary teardown on abort, lets in-flight finish
revisionHistoryLimit: 3 - allows quick rollback to recent prior versions

solution_files.content:
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: AnalysisTemplate
metadata:
  name: canary-slo
spec:
  args:
  - name: service
  - name: canary-hash
  metrics:
  - name: error-rate
    interval: 30s
    count: 4
    successCondition: result[0] < 0.005
    failureLimit: 1
    provider:
      prometheus:
        address: http://prometheus.observability.svc:9090
        query: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="{{args.service}}",pod_template_hash="{{args.canary-hash}}",status=~"5.."}[1m]))
          /
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="{{args.service}}",pod_template_hash="{{args.canary-hash}}"}[1m]))
  - name: p99-latency
    interval: 30s
    count: 4
    successCondition: result[0] < 0.2
    failureLimit: 1
    provider:
      prometheus:
        address: http://prometheus.observability.svc:9090
        query: |
          histogram_quantile(0.99,
            sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{service="{{args.service}}",pod_template_hash="{{args.canary-hash}}"}[1m])) by (le)
          )
  - name: traffic-volume-sanity
    interval: 30s
    count: 4
    successCondition: result[0] >= 10
    failureLimit: 4
    provider:
      prometheus:
        address: http://prometheus.observability.svc:9090
        query: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="{{args.service}}",pod_template_hash="{{args.canary-hash}}"}[1m]))
solution_files.purpose: SLO-based promotion gate: error rate, p99 latency, and minimum traffic sanity check
key_decisions: pod_template_hash label filters metrics to canary pods only - we judge canary on its own merit
count: 4, interval: 30s = 2-minute window - smooths over single spikes
traffic-volume-sanity prevents false promotion when canary gets near-zero traffic (e.g., misconfigured ingress)
failureLimit: 1 on SLO metrics - one bad sample triggers abort
Queries are PromQL - portable across Prometheus-compatible backends

solution_files.content:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: stream-api-ingress
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/ingress.class: alb
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/scheme: internet-facing
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/target-type: ip
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/listen-ports: '[{"HTTPS":443}]'
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-policy: ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/certificate-arn: arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123:certificate/abc
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/target-group-attributes: stickiness.enabled=true,stickiness.type=app_cookie,stickiness.app_cookie.cookie_name=ROUTING_AFFINITY,stickiness.app_cookie.duration_seconds=86400
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/load-balancer-attributes: idle_timeout.timeout_seconds=120
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/healthcheck-path: /healthz/ready
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/healthcheck-interval-seconds: '10'
spec:
  rules:
  - host: stream.acme.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: stream-api-stable
            port: {number: 80}
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: stream-api-canary
            port: {number: 80}
solution_files.purpose: Single ALB ingress with two backend services (stable + canary) for weight-based routing
key_decisions: App cookie stickiness with named cookie - app sets ROUTING_AFFINITY cookie, ALB honors it across stable/canary
idle_timeout 120s - longer than Node keepAliveTimeout 65s, prevents premature connection close
TLS 1.3 policy - modern, no SSLv3/TLS 1.0/1.1
Two backend services with same path - AWS LB Controller wires them as weighted targets per Rollouts spec

solution_files.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: argo-rollouts-notification-configmap
  namespace: argo-rollouts
data:
  service.slack: |
    token: $slack-token
  template.app-canary-started: |
    message: |
      :rocket: Canary started for {{.rollout.metadata.name}}
      Image: {{(index .rollout.spec.template.spec.containers 0).image}}
      Step: {{.rollout.status.currentStepIndex}}
      <https://argo.acme.com/rollouts/{{.rollout.metadata.namespace}}/{{.rollout.metadata.name}}|View>
    slack:
      blocks: |
        - type: section
          text:
            type: mrkdwn
            text: ":rocket: Canary started for *{{.rollout.metadata.name}}*"
  template.app-canary-aborted: |
    message: |
      :rotating_light: Canary ABORTED for {{.rollout.metadata.name}}
      Reason: {{.rollout.status.message}}
      Pages oncall immediately.
  trigger.on-rollout-step-completed: |
    - send: [app-canary-started]
      when: rollout.status.currentStepIndex != nil
  trigger.on-rollout-aborted: |
    - send: [app-canary-aborted]
      when: rollout.status.abort == true
  subscriptions: |
    - recipients:
      - slack:deploys
      triggers:
      - on-rollout-step-completed
    - recipients:
      - slack:incidents
      triggers:
      - on-rollout-aborted
solution_files.purpose: Argo Rollouts notifications routed to Slack on step transitions and aborts
key_decisions: Step transitions to #deploys (informational); aborts to #incidents (paging)
Token referenced as $slack-token - sourced from a Secret, not inline
Templates allow rich Slack blocks; deeper info via Argo UI link

security_considerations: Argo Rollouts runs with narrow RBAC: can only modify Rollouts, ReplicaSets, Services, Ingresses in target namespaces (not cluster-wide). Slack token in K8s Secret, mounted only into argo-rollouts-notifications-controller pod. Analysis queries Prometheus over in-cluster ClusterIP (no public exposure). Rollback is automatic on SLO breach - removes pressure for engineers to skip security review under deploy-day stress.
observability_hooks: Prometheus metric `rollout_phase{name,namespace,phase}` exposed by Argo Rollouts itself. Custom dashboard: canary error rate vs stable error rate (overlaid graph - reveals canary-only regressions instantly). Alert: rollout stuck in 'Paused' state for >2x expected duration - signals analysis ambiguity needing human review. CloudWatch: ALB target group HealthyHostCount per stable/canary - both should be >0 mid-rollout. DORA metrics emitted on rollout completion: change_failure_rate, deployment_frequency, lead_time, mean_time_to_recovery.
cost_analysis: Argo Rollouts controller: 1 pod, ~50m CPU + 64MiB - $5/mo. No traffic-routing cost increase (same ALB). Slack integration: $0. Potential savings: faster auto-rollback reduces customer-visible incident time; assuming 4 incidents/quarter * 8 min saved per * $20k/hr incident cost = ~$10k/quarter saved. Operationally: SRE no longer monitoring every deploy = ~5 engineer-hrs/week reclaimed.
rollback_procedure:
1. Automatic: AnalysisRun fails → Rollouts sets weight 0 on canary, scales canary RS to 0, status=Degraded. Within 60s of metric breach. No human action needed for the common case.
2. Manual abort during rollout: `kubectl argo rollouts abort stream-api -n prod` - same effect, instant.
3. Promote despite analysis warning: `kubectl argo rollouts promote stream-api --full` - skips remaining steps. Use sparingly with sign-off.
4. Roll back to N-1 after promotion: `kubectl argo rollouts undo stream-api -n prod` - flips stable to prior ReplicaSet (which is still alive due to revisionHistoryLimit=3).
5. Decision: trust the automation by default. Override only with documented rationale - the system has more samples and faster reflexes than a human.
runbook: Rollout stuck in 'Paused' (Analysis returned inconclusive): check Prometheus query manually with the same args; usually a metric label mismatch (e.g., service label name changed).
Promoting under flagged metric: get manager sign-off in #deploys, run `kubectl argo rollouts promote --skip-current-step <name>` - skips one step only.
Argo Rollouts controller down: existing Rollouts continue in last state; no automatic progression or rollback. Investigate controller pod logs; usually it's PVC or RBAC.
Adding a new SLO to AnalysisTemplate: PR with new metric block; deploy to staging first; observe one rollout to ensure threshold isn't too tight.
Force-rollback via Slack: type `/argo abort stream-api` in #deploys; bot calls kubectl on behalf with user identity logged.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior 'implements canary' by manually setting Deployment replicas to 38 of stable + 2 of canary, exposing both via the same Service. ALB load-balances roughly evenly (kube-proxy uses iptables round-robin, ignorant of pod_template_hash). Result: traffic to canary is closer to 50% than 5% because ALB doesn't know about the version difference. Worse, no session affinity means a user might hit v1 then v2 then v1 within the same session - WebSocket reconnects floods the canary pods, they crash from memory pressure, hard rollback. Postmortem: 'we thought we had a canary'. Reality: there's no canary without metric-driven traffic shaping. Argo Rollouts isn't optional infrastructure for progressive delivery - it's the actual implementation. Without it, you have hope, not control.

title: Harden a fresh Ubuntu 24.04 Hetzner VPS into a production Node.js host with Ansible
scenario_context: Bootstrapped SaaS, $450/mo infra budget. Founder runs Node.js + Postgres + nginx on a single CX42 Hetzner VPS (8 vCPU, 16GB, $30/mo). Server was set up by clicking around: root SSH password, no firewall, Postgres listening on 0.0.0.0, app runs as root via `node server.js` in a screen session. Found in shodan.io scan; fail2ban currently logging ~3k SSH brute-force attempts/day. Needs to harden + script the whole thing so they can rebuild in 30 minutes during a disaster.
requirements: Server rebuildable from Ansible playbook in <30 min
SSH key-only auth, root login disabled, custom port
UFW firewall: only 22 (custom port), 80, 443 inbound
fail2ban for SSH + nginx
Unattended security upgrades
App runs as non-root user via systemd unit
nginx reverse proxy with HTTP/2, Brotli, TLS 1.3, HSTS, Let's Encrypt auto-renew
Postgres listening on localhost only, automated daily backups to S3-compatible storage (Hetzner Object Storage)
Swap file sized appropriately, sysctl tuning for production Node.js
constraints: Single VPS - no clustering
Budget: $450/mo total (currently $30 VPS + must add backups)
Founder is not a sysadmin - playbook must be self-documenting
Must work for a clean rebuild on new VPS without manual steps

current_state.description: Hand-configured server, root SSH, no firewall, app runs as root, no backups, no automated TLS renewal.

files.content:
# what I did to set up the server
apt update && apt upgrade -y
apt install -y nodejs postgresql nginx
node /home/server.js &  # in a screen session
# nginx config: proxy_pass to localhost:3000, no TLS
# postgres: bind = 0.0.0.0, password = changeme123
issues: Postgres open to internet on default port
No process supervision (screen exits = app dies)

reasoning:
1. WHY IT FAILS
This is the typical 'works until it doesn't' setup. Each shortcut (root SSH, screen for daemonization, no firewall) is fine in isolation. Together they form a kill chain: anyone who finds the IP via Shodan brute-forces SSH (the 3k attempts/day is the appetizer), gets root, dumps Postgres via the open 5432, ransom's the data. The founder will discover this on a Monday morning.

Separately, lack of automation means a hardware failure or a 'oops rm -rf' is hours of frantic re-setup with no idea what was configured how. The disaster recovery story is 'panic'.

2. DESIGN DECISIONS
Ansible over shell scripts: idempotent, declarative, replayable. Single playbook with role-per-concern (ssh, firewall, postgres, nginx, app, backups). Inventory of one host today; grows naturally if they add a second.

SSH on custom port (22022) - not security but reduces noise from random scanners 90%; real attackers find it via nmap. Combined with key-only + fail2ban, brute-force is a non-issue.

UFW for simplicity (firewalld is fine but more knobs). Default deny inbound, allow 22022/tcp from anywhere (need remote access), 80/443 from anywhere.

Unattended upgrades configured for security updates only (not full distribution upgrades) - reboot scheduled at 4am if kernel update. Tradeoff: <5 min downtime/month vs months-old unpatched vulnerabilities.

App runs under a 'appuser' system user via systemd. systemd unit specifies `Restart=on-failure`, `RestartSec=5s`, resource limits, environment from EnvironmentFile (no secrets in unit). PID 1 is systemd-as-supervisor, not a screen session.

Postgres: bind to 127.0.0.1, password generated and stored in /etc/secrets/postgres.env (0600 root). Connection via localhost only. pg_hba.conf with scram-sha-256 not md5.

nginx: reverse proxy on 80→443 redirect, 443 with TLS 1.3-only + 1.2 fallback ciphers; HTTP/2; Brotli static + dynamic; HSTS preload; proper proxy headers (X-Real-IP, X-Forwarded-For, X-Forwarded-Proto); request size limits; rate limiting on /api/auth/*.

Let's Encrypt via certbot --nginx, auto-renew via systemd timer (already shipped by certbot).

Backups: pg_dump nightly + WAL archiving to Hetzner Object Storage (S3-compatible). pg_dump.sql.gz uploaded with date stamp. Retention: 30 daily, 12 monthly, 7 weekly via lifecycle. Total cost ~$3/mo.

Swap: 4GB swap file (Node memory leak insurance, not for normal use). swappiness=10 so kernel prefers RAM.

sysctl: net.core.somaxconn=1024, net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog=2048, vm.swappiness=10, fs.file-max=200000.

3. REJECTED ALTERNATIVES
- Docker for everything: adds operational complexity, founder isn't fluent.
- Kubernetes: absurd at this scale.
- Cloud-init script: brittle, not idempotent, hard to rerun.
- Bash setup script: same, plus no rollback.
- Managed Postgres (Hetzner doesn't have one; DigitalOcean does at $15/mo - viable later upgrade).
- Caddy instead of nginx: viable, simpler config. Stuck with nginx because the founder already knows ~half of it.

4. FAILURE MODES PREVENTED
- SSH brute-force: key-only + fail2ban + custom port.
- DB exfiltration via internet: bind to localhost.
- Container/process escape gives root: app runs as appuser, can't read /etc/shadow.
- App crash = downtime: systemd restarts.
- Cert expiry: certbot timer.
- Disk full from logs: logrotate.
- Server gone: rebuild from playbook + restore from backup in ~30 min.

5. MIGRATION PATH
Step 1: Spin up a new VPS, run Ansible from laptop. Verify everything green.
Step 2: Migrate DB: pg_dump from old VPS, restore to new.
Step 3: DNS cutover (5-min TTL prepared 1 day before).
Step 4: Tear down old VPS after 72h.
Step 5: Document Ansible repo in README.

6. OPERATIONAL CONSIDERATIONS
Ansible repo with vault-encrypted secrets (ansible-vault). Playbook committed to private git. Quarterly disaster drill: spin up fresh VPS, run playbook + restore backup, verify the site works. If anything fails, document the gap and fix the playbook.

target_architecture.description: Single Hetzner VPS managed by Ansible playbook. Hardened SSH, UFW firewall, fail2ban, unattended-upgrades. App runs as non-root systemd service behind nginx with TLS 1.3 + HTTP/2 + Brotli. Postgres localhost-only with nightly encrypted backups to Hetzner Object Storage.
target_architecture.diagram_description:
Internet → :80 nginx (HTTP→HTTPS redirect)
            → :443 nginx (TLS terminate, HTTP/2, Brotli)
              → 127.0.0.1:3000 Node.js app (systemd, user: appuser)
                → 127.0.0.1:5432 Postgres (localhost only)
Nightly: systemd timer → pg_dump | gzip | aws s3 cp s3://hetzner-backup/...
Unattended-upgrades: daily security patches, auto-reboot at 4am if kernel

solution_files.content:
---
- name: Bootstrap production VPS
  hosts: production
  become: true
  vars_files:
    - vars/secrets.yml
  roles:
    - role: base
      tags: base
    - role: ssh
      tags: ssh
    - role: firewall
      tags: firewall
    - role: fail2ban
      tags: fail2ban
    - role: swap
      tags: swap
    - role: sysctl
      tags: sysctl
    - role: unattended_upgrades
      tags: upgrades
    - role: node
      tags: node
    - role: postgres
      tags: postgres
    - role: app
      tags: app
    - role: nginx
      tags: nginx
    - role: certbot
      tags: certbot
    - role: backups
      tags: backups
solution_files.purpose: Top-level orchestration of all roles in dependency order
key_decisions: Roles ordered by dependency: ssh before firewall before fail2ban (which monitors logs)
Tags allow partial reruns: `ansible-playbook playbook.yml --tags=backups`
Secrets in ansible-vault, never in plaintext
become: true at play level - role-specific privilege escalation possible but defaults match needs

solution_files.content:
---
- name: Create deploy user
  ansible.builtin.user:
    name: deploy
    groups: sudo
    shell: /bin/bash
    create_home: true

- name: Add SSH key for deploy user
  ansible.posix.authorized_key:
    user: deploy
    state: present
    key: "{{ deploy_ssh_pubkey }}"

- name: Harden sshd_config
  ansible.builtin.copy:
    dest: /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/99-hardening.conf
    owner: root
    group: root
    mode: '0644'
    content: |
      Port {{ ssh_port }}
      Protocol 2
      PermitRootLogin no
      PasswordAuthentication no
      PubkeyAuthentication yes
      ChallengeResponseAuthentication no
      KbdInteractiveAuthentication no
      UsePAM yes
      X11Forwarding no
      AllowTcpForwarding no
      AllowAgentForwarding no
      ClientAliveInterval 300
      ClientAliveCountMax 2
      MaxAuthTries 3
      MaxSessions 5
      LoginGraceTime 30
      AllowUsers deploy
      KexAlgorithms curve25519-sha256@libssh.org,curve25519-sha256
      Ciphers chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com,aes256-gcm@openssh.com
      MACs hmac-sha2-512-etm@openssh.com,hmac-sha2-256-etm@openssh.com
  notify: restart ssh
  validate: /usr/sbin/sshd -t -f %s

- name: Allow sudo without password for deploy
  ansible.builtin.copy:
    dest: /etc/sudoers.d/deploy
    owner: root
    group: root
    mode: '0440'
    content: 'deploy ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL'
    validate: /usr/sbin/visudo -cf %s
solution_files.purpose: SSH hardening: key-only, modern crypto, custom port, deploy user with sudo
key_decisions: sshd_config.d/ instead of editing main file - survives package updates
validate: hooks fail closed - bad config never reloaded
Modern KEX/Cipher/MAC - rejects legacy clients with known weaknesses
ClientAliveInterval prevents idle session hijack
NOPASSWD sudo is a trade-off: convenient for automation, fine because access already gated by SSH key + 2FA on the deploy laptop

solution_files.content:
---
- name: Install UFW
  ansible.builtin.apt:
    name: ufw
    state: present

- name: Default deny incoming
  community.general.ufw:
    default: deny
    direction: incoming

- name: Default allow outgoing
  community.general.ufw:
    default: allow
    direction: outgoing

- name: Allow SSH on custom port
  community.general.ufw:
    rule: limit
    port: "{{ ssh_port }}"
    proto: tcp
    comment: SSH rate-limited

- name: Allow HTTP
  community.general.ufw:
    rule: allow
    port: '80'
    proto: tcp

- name: Allow HTTPS
  community.general.ufw:
    rule: allow
    port: '443'
    proto: tcp

- name: Enable UFW
  community.general.ufw:
    state: enabled
    logging: 'low'
solution_files.purpose: UFW with default deny, SSH on custom port rate-limited, web ports open
key_decisions: limit on SSH = ufw rate-limits to 6 connections/30s per IP - cheap brute-force defense
logging: low keeps /var/log/ufw.log small but catches blocks
Default deny incoming - explicit allows only

solution_files.content:
[Unit]
Description=Acme Node API
After=network.target postgresql.service
Wants=postgresql.service

[Service]
Type=simple
User=appuser
Group=appuser
WorkingDirectory=/opt/app/current
EnvironmentFile=/etc/secrets/app.env
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node --enable-source-maps dist/server.js
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5s
StandardOutput=append:/var/log/app/app.log
StandardError=append:/var/log/app/app.err

# Hardening
NoNewPrivileges=yes
PrivateTmp=yes
ProtectSystem=strict
ProtectHome=yes
ReadWritePaths=/var/log/app /opt/app/current/uploads
ProtectKernelTunables=yes
ProtectKernelModules=yes
ProtectControlGroups=yes
RestrictSUIDSGID=yes
LockPersonality=yes
MemoryDenyWriteExecute=no
RestrictRealtime=yes
SystemCallArchitectures=native

# Limits
LimitNOFILE=65535
MemoryMax=12G
CPUQuota=700%

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
solution_files.purpose: systemd unit running Node.js as non-root with sandboxing
key_decisions: After=network.target postgresql.service - won't start until deps ready
EnvironmentFile loads secrets at start, mode 0600 root-owned
ProtectSystem=strict + ReadWritePaths - app can only write to log dir and uploads
LimitNOFILE=65535 - high-concurrency Node.js needs file descriptors
MemoryMax=12G of the 16G total - leaves room for Postgres and OS
Restart=on-failure with RestartSec=5s - 5-second backoff prevents tight crash loops
MemoryDenyWriteExecute=no because Node.js JIT needs RWX pages

solution_files.purpose: nginx reverse proxy with TLS 1.3 + HTTP/2 + Brotli + security headers + rate limiting
key_decisions: TLS 1.3 + 1.2 fallback - covers ~99.5% of clients without legacy crypto
HSTS preload-ready - 2-year max-age, includeSubDomains
Brotli for compressible types; gzip fallback for old clients
Per-zone rate limits: auth endpoints stricter than general API
client_max_body_size 25M - tune for actual upload sizes
Proxy upgrade headers - supports WebSocket
ssl_session_tickets off - prefer forward secrecy via session_cache

solution_files.content:
---
- name: Install awscli
  ansible.builtin.apt:
    name: awscli
    state: present

- name: Install backup script
  ansible.builtin.copy:
    dest: /usr/local/bin/pg-backup.sh
    owner: root
    group: root
    mode: '0750'
    content: |
      #!/bin/bash
      set -euo pipefail
      umask 077
      export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID={{ hetzner_s3_key_id }}
      export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY={{ hetzner_s3_secret }}
      export AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=eu-central-1
      STAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
      DUMP=/var/backups/db-$STAMP.sql.gz
      pg_dump -h 127.0.0.1 -U postgres -F c app | gzip -9 > $DUMP
      SHA=$(sha256sum $DUMP | cut -d' ' -f1)
      aws --endpoint-url https://nbg1.your-objectstorage.com s3 cp $DUMP s3://acme-backups/db/$STAMP.sql.gz --metadata sha256=$SHA
      echo "backup_complete=$STAMP sha256=$SHA size_bytes=$(stat -c %s $DUMP)"
      find /var/backups -name 'db-*.sql.gz' -mtime +3 -delete

- name: Backup systemd timer
  ansible.builtin.copy:
    dest: /etc/systemd/system/pg-backup.timer
    content: |
      [Unit]
      Description=Daily Postgres backup
      [Timer]
      OnCalendar=*-*-* 02:30:00
      RandomizedDelaySec=600
      Persistent=true
      [Install]
      WantedBy=timers.target

- name: Backup systemd service
  ansible.builtin.copy:
    dest: /etc/systemd/system/pg-backup.service
    content: |
      [Unit]
      Description=Postgres backup
      [Service]
      Type=oneshot
      User=postgres
      ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/pg-backup.sh
      StandardOutput=journal
      StandardError=journal

- name: Enable backup timer
  ansible.builtin.systemd:
    name: pg-backup.timer
    enabled: true
    state: started
    daemon_reload: true

- name: Weekly restore drill timer
  ansible.builtin.copy:
    dest: /etc/systemd/system/restore-drill.timer
    content: |
      [Unit]
      Description=Weekly backup restore verification
      [Timer]
      OnCalendar=Sun 04:00:00
      Persistent=true
      [Install]
      WantedBy=timers.target
solution_files.purpose: Daily encrypted backup to Hetzner Object Storage with SHA verification and weekly restore drill
key_decisions: sha256 stored as object metadata - integrity verifiable on restore
pg_dump -F c (custom format) - parallelizable restore, compresses better
RandomizedDelaySec spreads load if many VPSs do this
Weekly restore drill is the real safety net - untested backup is not a backup
Local 3-day retention for fast restore; longer history in S3

security_considerations: SSH key-only on a custom port reduces brute-force log noise 99%; combined with fail2ban + UFW limit, brute-force is non-viable. App runs as appuser with systemd sandboxing - container-level isolation without Docker. Postgres on localhost cannot be reached externally even if a vulnerable pg version. Backup credentials separate from app credentials - app cannot exfiltrate the backup bucket. Secrets in /etc/secrets/ at 0600 root, sourced into systemd environment file - never in process listing or git. Unattended security upgrades + monthly reboot if kernel patched closes the patch-lag window most VPSs suffer.
observability_hooks: systemd journal aggregates all service logs - `journalctl -u app -f` for live tail. node_exporter installed via base role exposes host metrics to a remote Prometheus (Hetzner has $9/mo managed Grafana Cloud). Alert on: disk_free < 10%, memory used > 90%, swap_in > 0 (signals memory pressure), SSH failed_logins > 100/min (brute-force burst), backup job failure (last_success_timestamp), nginx 5xx rate > 1%. Backup script logs structured `backup_complete=... sha256=... size_bytes=...` lines - parse for backup size growth trend (catches DB bloat).
cost_analysis: VPS: $30/mo (existing). Hetzner Object Storage: $2.50/mo for 100GB + minimal egress. Grafana Cloud free tier covers 10k metrics and 50GB logs - $0 to start. Total: ~$33/mo, well under $450 budget. Headroom for: managed Postgres ($15-30/mo) when scaling, second VPS for HA ($30/mo), CDN like Bunny.net ($1-5/mo). No cost for hardening - it's all OSS and config.
rollback_procedure:
1. Bad Ansible run: re-run prior commit's playbook. Idempotent design means most changes revert cleanly.
2. Bad app deploy: app is deployed via `/opt/app/releases/<sha>` symlinked to `/opt/app/current`. Rollback = `ln -sfn /opt/app/releases/<prior-sha> /opt/app/current && systemctl restart app`. 3-second rollback.
3. Bad sshd_config: validate: hook prevents reload of broken config. If somehow shipped, console access via Hetzner web UI.
4. Bad nginx config: `nginx -t` validated before reload. If shipped, hetzner-rescue boot + edit /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/.
5. Lost VPS entirely: provision new VPS, run playbook (~25 min), restore latest backup (~5 min for typical 2GB DB), update DNS A record. Tested quarterly.
runbook: Restoring a backup: `aws --endpoint-url=... s3 cp s3://acme-backups/db/<STAMP>.sql.gz - | gunzip | pg_restore -d app` from the new VPS.
Adding a TLS cert renewal hook: certbot deploys hooks in /etc/letsencrypt/renewal-hooks/deploy/ - any executable there runs after renewal. Use to reload nginx.
Investigating high SSH attack volume: `fail2ban-client status sshd` shows banned IPs; if log noise overwhelming, raise findtime/maxretry temporarily.
Emergency rotate Postgres password: edit `/etc/secrets/postgres.env` + `/etc/postgresql/16/main/pg_hba.conf`; restart pg; restart app.
Disk-full incident: check /var/log/ for runaway logs, /var/backups for backup retention overrun, /var/lib/postgresql for DB bloat (VACUUM FULL after backup if safe).
what_breaks_naive_approach: The naive setup IS the current state. Three months in, fail2ban quietly OOMs because nobody set memory limits and it's tracking 3k IPs. SSH brute-force succeeds via a leaked SSH key from a former contractor. Attacker dumps the open-port Postgres in 12 seconds (it's small), encrypts files, demands 0.5 BTC. Founder pays because there's no backup (or 'I think I copied something to my laptop last month'). Postmortem: every individual gap was 'I'll get to it'. With Ansible playbook + quarterly drill, this scenario is closed and provably so.

title: Stand up Prometheus + Grafana + Loki + Tempo on EKS with meaningful dashboards and SLO-based alerts
scenario_context: Series B B2B SaaS, 22 microservices on EKS. Currently 'observability' = CloudWatch Logs Insights queries and tribal knowledge. MTTD for production issues is 14 min; MTTR is 47 min. No tracing - debugging cross-service issues means greping 22 log groups simultaneously. Alerting is a pile of CloudWatch alarms that page on CPU >80% (useless - mostly noise). SRE team wants real observability: RED metrics per service, distributed traces, structured logs, SLO-based alerts.
requirements: Prometheus scrapes all services + kube-state-metrics + node-exporter
Grafana dashboards: per-service RED, infra overview, SLO burn-rate
Loki for log aggregation, structured JSON, 30-day retention
Tempo for distributed traces with OpenTelemetry SDKs
Alerts: multi-window multi-burn-rate (Google SRE book), not threshold-based
All telemetry retained 30 days hot, 1 year cold
Cost cap: $400/mo for the observability stack itself
constraints: Must self-host (existing security review process for SaaS observability vendors is 6 months)
Cannot disrupt existing CloudWatch (legacy alarms migrate over time)
SDK changes to apps allowed but minimal
Cluster has 40 nodes, 600 pods, ~5M log lines/day

current_state.description: Each service ships logs to CloudWatch via FluentBit DaemonSet. No metrics beyond CloudWatch infra defaults. No traces. Alarms set per-service via Terraform but mostly fire on CPU.

files.content:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "api_cpu" {
  alarm_name = "api-high-cpu"
  metric_name = "CPUUtilization"
  threshold = 80
  period = 60
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods = 2
}
issues: CPU is not a user-facing signal
Fires constantly during normal load spikes
No way to know if customer requests are affected
No correlation to deployments or other services

reasoning:
1. WHY THIS FAILS
CPU-based alerts are a category error: they tell you what the machine is doing, not what the user is experiencing. A service can be at 95% CPU serving traffic perfectly, or at 5% CPU returning 100% errors. Alerting on infra metrics is a 2010-era pattern.

The 14-min MTTD comes from this: alarm fires on CPU; on-call gets paged; checks CloudWatch; sees CPU was a spike; resolves the alarm; meanwhile real issue (a downstream service returning 503s) went undetected. By the time customer complaints route through CS to engineering, 30+ min are gone.

No tracing means cross-service debugging is correlation-by-timestamp across 22 log groups. Add network jitter and clock skew, and you're effectively guessing.

2. DESIGN DECISIONS
Self-host Prometheus + Grafana + Loki + Tempo (the 'PLG' or 'LGTM' stack from Grafana Labs). All OSS. Single Grafana frontend datasourcing all three. Deployed via kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart for Prom+Grafana, separate loki-stack and tempo-distributed charts.

Prometheus: 1 replica with 100Gi PV for hot data (15 days), remote-write to Thanos or just rely on snapshot-to-S3 for long-term. At 600 pods x ~30 metric series each = 18k series. With 15s scrape interval, ~30GB/month - fits comfortably.

Loki: scaled-down distributed mode (compactor, distributor, ingester, querier). Object storage (S3) for chunks, BoltDB for index. 5M lines/day at avg 500 bytes = 2.5GB/day, ~75GB/month. S3 lifecycle to Glacier after 30 days.

Tempo: object storage backend (S3). Head-based sampling at 5% in apps (or 100% for low-volume services). Tail-based sampling via tempo's pipeline for 'all error traces, all slow traces, 5% of normal'.

OpenTelemetry SDKs in apps: auto-instrumentation for Express, pg, redis, http. Custom spans for business logic. Single OTEL collector DaemonSet receives OTLP from apps, exports metrics→Prom (via remote write), logs→Loki, traces→Tempo.

Alerts: replace threshold alerts with SLO-based multi-window burn-rate (the SRE Workbook chapter). For each service we define an SLO (e.g., 99.9% availability over 30 days). Alert fires when the error budget burn rate exceeds 14.4x over a 1-hour window AND 6x over a 5-min window (page) or 3x/1h+1x/6h (ticket). This catches both fast-burn (sudden incidents) and slow-burn (degradation over hours) without paging on normal noise.

Dashboards: RED method per service (Request rate, Error rate, Duration p50/p95/p99). One row per service, one panel per signal, on a single 'Overview' dashboard. Deep-dive dashboard per service linked from each row.

3. REJECTED ALTERNATIVES
- Datadog: $200-500/host/month at this scale = $8k-20k/mo, way over budget.
- New Relic / Honeycomb / Lightstep: similar budget problem.
- Continuing CloudWatch-only: doesn't solve the lack of metrics/traces/structured logs.
- Elastic stack (ELK): heavier ops burden, Loki is leaner for log-only.
- Cortex/Mimir for Prom HA: needed at 10x our scale; single Prom suffices now.

4. FAILURE MODES PREVENTED
- Alert fatigue: SLO-based alerts only fire when user-visible degradation is meaningful. Expect <2 pages/week vs current ~15/week.
- Slow MTTD: SLO violations fire within 5 min of breach (vs current 14 min).
- Cross-service debugging blind spots: a trace shows the entire request path with timing per span.
- Stack lock-in: OTEL is vendor-neutral; can swap backends without changing SDKs.
- Storage cost runaway: object-store backends with lifecycle policies.

5. MIGRATION PATH
Week 1: Helm install kube-prometheus-stack. Default scrape config gets node + kubelet + cAdvisor + kube-state-metrics. Verify basic infra dashboards.
Week 2: Add ServiceMonitors for each app (one PR per service team adds prom-client + /metrics endpoint).
Week 3: Loki + Promtail DaemonSet. FluentBit → Promtail migration in parallel for 2 weeks; cut over once Loki query patterns work.
Week 4: Tempo + OTEL collector. Add OTEL SDK to one service as pilot; verify trace flow end-to-end.
Week 5-8: Roll out OTEL to remaining 21 services. Build per-service dashboards from template.
Week 9-10: Define SLOs per service with product team. Build burn-rate alerts. Disable old CloudWatch CPU alarms after 1 week parallel run.

6. OPERATIONAL CONSIDERATIONS
SRE team owns the stack itself + the alert templates. Service teams own their SLOs and dashboards (template instantiated via Jsonnet/Grafonnet). On-call rotation now uses 'SLO violation' as the primary signal.

target_architecture.description: Prometheus scrapes targets discovered via Kubernetes service discovery + ServiceMonitor CRDs. Grafana queries Prom + Loki + Tempo as three datasources, presented unified. OTEL collector DaemonSet ingests from apps, fans out to backends. Long-term storage in S3. AlertManager fires multi-burn-rate alerts to PagerDuty.
target_architecture.diagram_description:
Apps (OTEL SDK) ──OTLP──► OTEL Collector DS ──► Prom remote_write
                                              ──► Loki push
                                              ──► Tempo OTLP
Prometheus ──scrape──► targets (ServiceMonitor)
Promtail DS ──► Loki
Prometheus ──snapshot──► S3 (long-term)
Loki ──chunks──► S3 (+ Glacier lifecycle)
Tempo ──blocks──► S3
Grafana ──► Prom + Loki + Tempo
AlertManager ──multi-burn-rate──► PagerDuty
